title: To Be (am / is / are)
explanation:
The verb 'to be' is one of the most important verbs in English. It has three forms in the present: am, is, and are.

Use 'am' with I.
Use 'is' with he, she, it, and singular nouns.
Use 'are' with you, we, they, and plural nouns.

To make a negative sentence, add 'not' after the verb: am not, is not (isn't), are not (aren't).

To make a question, put the verb before the subject: Am I...? Is he...? Are you...?

examples.sentence: I am a student.
She is from Tashkent.
They are my friends.
He is not happy today.
Are you ready?

title: Personal Pronouns (I, you, he, she, it, we, they)
explanation:
Personal pronouns replace nouns so we don't repeat names. 

I = the speaker. You = the listener. He = a male person. She = a female person. It = a thing or animal. We = the speaker and others. They = other people or things.

We use these pronouns as the subject of a sentence, before the verb.

examples.sentence: John is tired. He wants to sleep.
Maria is my friend. She lives near me.
The book is on the table. It is blue.
My brother and I are happy. We are happy.
Tom and Anna are students. They study English.

questions.text: Peter is my friend. ___ is very funny.
Sara is a doctor. ___ works at a hospital.
My cat is small. ___ likes to play.
My friends and I are at school. ___ are studying.
Ali and Vali are brothers. ___ live together.
Excuse me, Mr. Smith, are ___ busy?
The children are playing. ___ are happy.
This is my mother. ___ is a kind person.
My dog and I go for a walk every day. ___ enjoy it.

title: Articles: a, an, the
explanation:
We use 'a' and 'an' before singular, countable nouns when we mention something for the first time, or when it isn't specific.

Use 'a' before a consonant sound: a book, a car, a university.
Use 'an' before a vowel sound: an apple, an hour, an idea.

We use 'the' when we talk about a specific thing, something already mentioned, or something unique (like the sun, the moon).

We usually don't use an article with plural nouns in general statements, or with most proper names.

examples.sentence: I have a dog.
She is an engineer.
I saw a man. The man was tall.
The sun rises in the east.
Cats are independent animals.
examples.note: no article for general plural statement

questions.text: I bought ___ apple at the market.
I need ___ hour to finish this.
There is ___ cat in the garden.
He works at ___ university near here.
I want to buy ___ orange and a banana.
We visited ___ museum yesterday. ___ museum was huge.

title: Singular and Plural Nouns
explanation:
Most English nouns become plural by adding -s: book -> books, car -> cars.

If a noun ends in -s, -x, -ch, -sh, add -es: bus -> buses, box -> boxes, watch -> watches.

If a noun ends in a consonant + y, change y to i and add -es: city -> cities, baby -> babies.

Some nouns are irregular and don't follow these rules: man -> men, woman -> women, child -> children, person -> people, foot -> feet, tooth -> teeth.

Some nouns have no plural form at all, like water, information, advice.

examples.sentence: I have two books.
There are five buses outside.
She visited three cities.
There are many children in the park.
Many people like this song.

questions.text: There are three ___ (box) on the table.
There are many ___ (woman) in this photo.
We saw five ___ (man) at the market.
There are ten ___ (watch) in the shop.

title: This, That, These, Those
explanation:
We use 'this' and 'that' to point to one thing (singular). We use 'these' and 'those' to point to more than one thing (plural).

'This' and 'these' refer to things that are near us.
'That' and 'those' refer to things that are far from us.

We can use these words before a noun (this book) or alone, replacing the noun (This is my book).

examples.sentence: This is my phone.
That is your house, over there.
These are my shoes.
Those are beautiful mountains.
I like this book, not that one.

questions.text: ___ is my pen. (pointing to something near you, one item)
Look at ___ mountains over there. (far, plural)
___ car, over there, is very old. (far, singular)
Can I have one of ___ apples? (far, plural)
Whose are ___ books on the table near you?

title: Possessive Adjectives (my, your, his, her, its, our, their)
explanation:
Possessive adjectives show who something belongs to. They come before a noun.

I -> my, You -> your, He -> his, She -> her, It -> its, We -> our, They -> their.

Unlike some other languages, possessive adjectives in English do not change for plural nouns: 'my book' and 'my books' both use 'my'.

examples.sentence: This is my house.
Is this your bag?
He loves his job.
She visited her grandmother.
The cat is eating its food.

title: Possessive 's (Apostrophe S)
explanation:
We use 's after a singular noun to show possession: the girl's book (the book belongs to the girl).

For plural nouns ending in -s, we add only an apostrophe: the students' books.

For irregular plural nouns that don't end in -s (like children, men, women), we add 's: the children's toys.

We do not use 's for things - instead we usually use 'of' or just put the noun before another noun: the door of the car, or the car door.

examples.sentence: This is Anna's bag.
The boys' room is upstairs.
The children's playground is closed.
My sister's car is red.
The teachers' meeting starts at 5.

questions.text: The ___ books are on the table. (students, plural)
The ___ room is very clean. (boys, plural)
The ___ office is on the second floor. (manager)
The ___ shoes were left at the door. (women)

title: Present Simple (Affirmative)
explanation:
We use the Present Simple for habits, routines, facts, and things that are generally true.

For I, you, we, they, we use the base form of the verb: I work, you play, they live.

For he, she, it, we add -s to the verb: he works, she plays, it lives.

Verbs ending in -ch, -sh, -ss, -x, -o add -es: he watches, she goes.

Verbs ending in consonant + y change y to i and add -es: study -> studies.

examples.sentence: I work in a bank.
She works in a hospital.
He watches TV every evening.
They live in Samarkand.
My brother studies English.

questions.text: He ___ (go) to the gym every day.

title: Present Simple: Negative and Questions
explanation:
To make a negative sentence in the Present Simple, we use 'do not' (don't) or 'does not' (doesn't) before the base form of the verb.

Use don't with I, you, we, they: I don't like coffee.
Use doesn't with he, she, it: She doesn't like coffee.

To make a question, we put Do or Does at the beginning of the sentence: Do you like coffee? Does she like coffee?

Important: after don't/doesn't and Do/Does, the verb always stays in its base form, without -s.

examples.sentence: I don't eat meat.
She doesn't like loud music.
Do you speak English?
Does he work on Sundays?
They don't live here anymore.

title: Adverbs of Frequency (always, usually, often, sometimes, rarely, never)
explanation:
Adverbs of frequency tell us how often something happens. Common ones, from most to least frequent: always, usually, often, sometimes, rarely, never.

These adverbs usually come before the main verb: She always drinks tea in the morning.

But with the verb 'to be', the adverb comes after it: He is always late.

In questions, we often use 'How often...?' to ask about frequency: How often do you exercise?

examples.sentence: I always wake up at seven.
He is often busy on Mondays.
We sometimes go to the cinema.
She never drinks coffee.
How often do you read books?

questions.text: I ___ go to bed early. (100% of the time)
She ___ eats vegetables. (0% of the time)
___ often do you go to the gym?
Choose the correct sentence with 'to be'.

title: Present Continuous (am/is/are + -ing)
explanation:
We use the Present Continuous for actions happening right now, or around the present time.

Form: am/is/are + verb-ing. I am working, she is reading, they are playing.

Most verbs simply add -ing: play -> playing, read -> reading.
Verbs ending in -e usually drop the e: make -> making, write -> writing.
Short verbs ending in one vowel + one consonant double the last letter: run -> running, sit -> sitting.

We also use the Present Continuous for future arrangements: I am meeting her tomorrow.

examples.sentence: I am studying English now.
She is writing an email.
They are running in the park.
He is not sleeping right now.
Are you listening to me?

questions.text: She ___ (read) a book at the moment.

options: She is sit on the chair.
She is sitting on the chair.
questions.correctAnswer: She is sitting on the chair.

title: Present Simple vs Present Continuous
explanation:
Present Simple is for habits, routines, facts, and permanent situations: I work in a bank. Water boils at 100 degrees.

Present Continuous is for actions happening right now, or temporary situations: I am working right now. She is staying with friends this week.

Some words signal which tense to use: words like 'always, usually, every day' often go with Present Simple, while 'now, right now, at the moment' go with Present Continuous.

Some verbs (like, want, know, believe) are not normally used in the continuous form: we say 'I like it', not 'I am liking it'.

examples.sentence: I usually walk to school.
I am walking to school right now.
She works at a hospital.
She is working late this week.
I know the answer.
examples.note: 'know' is not used in continuous form

questions.text: At the moment, she ___ (study) for her exam.
The sun ___ (rise) in the east. (general fact)

title: There is / There are
explanation:
We use 'There is' with singular and uncountable nouns, and 'There are' with plural nouns, to say that something exists or is present in a place.

There is a book on the table. There are three books on the table.

Negative: There isn't / There aren't. Question: Is there...? Are there...?

We often use 'There is/are' with words like 'a', 'some', 'any', and numbers.

examples.sentence: There is a cat in the garden.
There are five chairs in the room.
There isn't any milk in the fridge.
Is there a bank near here?
Are there any students in the classroom?

questions.text: ___ a good restaurant near your house?
___ a lot of traffic this morning.
How many chairs ___ in the room?

title: Prepositions of Place (in, on, at, under, behind, next to)
explanation:
Prepositions of place show where something or someone is.

'In' is for enclosed spaces: in a box, in a room, in a city.
'On' is for surfaces: on the table, on the wall, on the floor.
'At' is for specific points or places: at the door, at the bus stop, at school.

Other useful prepositions: under (below something), behind (at the back of something), in front of (at the front of something), next to / beside (close to something), between (in the middle of two things).

examples.sentence: The keys are in my bag.
The book is on the table.
She is waiting at the bus stop.
The cat is under the bed.
The school is between the bank and the park.

questions.text: The cat is hiding ___ the sofa. (below)
The car is parked ___ the house. (close to)
There is a picture ___ the wall.
The garden is ___ the house. (at the back)
The bank is ___ the supermarket and the pharmacy.

title: Can / Can't (Ability)
explanation:
We use 'can' to talk about ability - things we are able to do.

Can is a modal verb: it doesn't change form, and we don't add -s for he/she/it. She can swim. NOT: She cans swim.

Negative: cannot or can't. I can't drive.

Question: Can + subject + verb? Can you speak English?

After can/can't, we always use the base form of the verb, without 'to'.

examples.sentence: I can swim very well.
She can speak three languages.
He can't drive a car.
Can you ride a bicycle?
They can play the piano.

questions.text: They ___ (can, negative) come to the party.
Birds ___ fly, but they can't talk.
We ___ see the mountains from here.

title: Past Simple: to be (was / were)
explanation:
The past form of 'to be' has two forms: was and were.

Use 'was' with I, he, she, it: I was tired. She was happy.
Use 'were' with you, we, they: You were late. They were at home.

Negative: was not (wasn't), were not (weren't).

Question: Was + subject...? Were + subject...? Was she ready? Were they at school?

examples.sentence: I was at home yesterday.
She was a teacher last year.
We were on holiday last week.
They weren't at the party.
Were you busy yesterday?

questions.text: They ___ very happy about the news.
She ___ not at home last night.

title: Past Simple: Regular Verbs
explanation:
Regular verbs form the past simple by adding -ed to the base form: work -> worked, play -> played.

If the verb ends in -e, just add -d: live -> lived, like -> liked.

If the verb ends in consonant + y, change y to i and add -ed: study -> studied.

Short verbs ending in one vowel + one consonant double the last letter: stop -> stopped.

The Past Simple form is the same for all subjects: I worked, you worked, he worked, they worked.

examples.sentence: I worked late yesterday.
She lived in London for two years.
He studied hard for the exam.
They stopped at the red light.
We played football last Sunday.

questions.text: What is the past form of 'stop'?
What is the past form of 'live'?
What is the past form of 'carry'?
She ___ (open) the window because it was hot.

title: Past Simple: Irregular Verbs
explanation:
Many common English verbs don't follow the -ed rule. These are called irregular verbs, and their past forms must be memorized.

Some common examples: go -> went, have -> had, do -> did, see -> saw, eat -> ate, make -> made, take -> took, come -> came, write -> wrote, read -> read (same spelling, different pronunciation).

Like regular verbs, the irregular past form is the same for all subjects: I went, she went, they went.

examples.sentence: I went to the market yesterday.
She had a great time at the party.
We saw a beautiful sunset.
He made a delicious cake.
They came home late last night.

questions.text: What is the past form of 'see'?
What is the past form of 'write'?
What is the past form of 'make'?
What is the past form of 'go'?

title: Past Simple: Negative and Questions
explanation:
To make a negative sentence in the Past Simple, we use 'did not' (didn't) before the base form of the verb - for both regular and irregular verbs.

I didn't go. She didn't work. They didn't see it.

To make a question, we put 'Did' at the beginning: Did you go? Did she work?

Important: after didn't and Did, the verb is always in its base form - never the past form. We say 'Did you go?', NOT 'Did you went?'.

examples.sentence: I didn't go to the party.
She didn't finish her homework.
Did you see that movie?
Did they come to the meeting?
We didn't have time yesterday.

questions.text: They ___ (not come) to the party last night.
___ they finish the project on time?

title: Past Continuous (was/were + -ing)
explanation:
We use the Past Continuous for actions that were in progress at a specific time in the past.

Form: was/were + verb-ing. I was working, they were sleeping.

We often use the Past Continuous with the Past Simple to show that one action interrupted another: I was watching TV when the phone rang.

We also use it to describe the background or scene of a story: It was raining, and people were walking quickly.

examples.sentence: I was sleeping when you called.
She was cooking dinner at 7 pm.
examples.note: action in progress at a specific time

examples.sentence: They were watching a movie last night.
It was raining when we left the house.
What were you doing yesterday at noon?

questions.text: They ___ (play) football when it started to rain.
What ___ you doing at 9 pm last night?
He ___ (not listen) when I explained the rule.

options: It was raining when we left.
It rained when we were leaving.
It was rain when we left.
questions.correctAnswer: It was raining when we left.

questions.text: I ___ (read) a book when the lights went out.
She ___ (walk) home when she saw an old friend.

title: Prepositions of Time (in, on, at)
explanation:
Prepositions of time tell us when something happens.

'In' is used for months, years, seasons, and longer periods: in May, in 2020, in summer, in the morning.

'On' is used for specific days and dates: on Monday, on my birthday, on 5th May.

'At' is used for specific times and certain fixed expressions: at 7 o'clock, at night, at the weekend, at Christmas.

examples.sentence: I was born in 2005.
We have class on Mondays.
The meeting starts at 9 o'clock.
She studies in the evening.
They go to the village at the weekend.

questions.text: We don't have school ___ the weekend.

title: Adverbs of Manner (quickly, well, carefully, slowly)
explanation:
Adverbs of manner describe how an action happens. They often answer the question 'How?'

Most adverbs of manner are formed by adding -ly to an adjective: quick -> quickly, slow -> slowly, careful -> carefully.

Some adverbs are irregular: good -> well, fast -> fast (no change).

Adverbs of manner usually come after the verb, or after the object if there is one: She speaks English fluently. He drives carefully.

examples.sentence: She speaks English fluently.
He drives very carefully.
They walked slowly through the park.
My sister sings well.
He runs fast.

questions.text: What is the adverb form of 'careful'?

title: Comparative Adjectives (bigger, more interesting)
explanation:
We use comparative adjectives to compare two things.

Short adjectives (one syllable) add -er: tall -> taller, big -> bigger (double the consonant).

Adjectives ending in -y change y to i and add -er: happy -> happier.

Longer adjectives (two or more syllables) use 'more' + adjective: more interesting, more beautiful.

We use 'than' to compare two things: She is taller than her brother.

Some adjectives are irregular: good -> better, bad -> worse, far -> farther/further.

examples.sentence: My brother is taller than me.
This book is more interesting than that one.
Today is hotter than yesterday.
She is happier than before.
This test was worse than the last one.

questions.text: This box is ___ (big) than that one.
This movie is ___ (interesting) than the last one.

options: He is more tall than me.
He is more taller than me.

questions.text: My old car was ___ (bad) than my new one.
This city is ___ (big) than my hometown.

title: Superlative Adjectives (the biggest, the most interesting)
explanation:
We use superlative adjectives to compare three or more things, to say one is the extreme example.

Short adjectives add -est: tall -> the tallest, big -> the biggest (double consonant).

Adjectives ending in -y change y to i and add -est: happy -> the happiest.

Longer adjectives use 'the most' + adjective: the most interesting, the most beautiful.

We always use 'the' before a superlative.

Irregular forms: good -> the best, bad -> the worst, far -> the farthest/furthest.

examples.sentence: She is the tallest student in the class.
This is the most beautiful place I've seen.
Today is the hottest day of the year.
He is the best player on the team.
That was the worst movie I've ever watched.

questions.text: She is the ___ (tall) girl in the class.
This is the ___ (interesting) book I've read.
Everest is the ___ (high) mountain in the world.
This is the ___ (expensive) restaurant in the city.

options: He is the most tall in his family.
He is the tallest in his family.
He is most tallest in his family.
questions.correctAnswer: He is the tallest in his family.

questions.text: January is the ___ (cold) month of the year here.
She is the ___ (happy) person I know.
This was the ___ exam of the year.

title: Future: going to
explanation:
We use 'going to' to talk about plans and intentions for the future - things we have already decided to do.

Form: am/is/are + going to + base verb. I am going to study tonight. She is going to visit her parents.

We also use 'going to' to make predictions based on what we can see now: Look at those clouds! It's going to rain.

Negative: am/is/are + not + going to. Question: Am/Is/Are + subject + going to...?

examples.sentence: I am going to start a new job next month.
She is going to study medicine.
Look at the sky! It's going to rain.
We are not going to travel this summer.
Are you going to come to the party?

questions.text: She ___ (going to / visit) her grandmother tomorrow.
Look at those dark clouds! It ___ rain.
We ___ (going to / travel) to Samarkand next week.
___ you going to attend the meeting?
He ___ (not / going to / come) to the party.
They ___ (going to / build) a new house.
What ___ you going to do this weekend?
She ___ (going to / be) a doctor in the future.

title: Future: will
explanation:
We use 'will' for predictions, promises, offers, and decisions made at the moment of speaking.

Form: will + base verb (the same for all subjects). I will help you. She will call later.

Negative: will not (won't). I won't be late.

Question: Will + subject + verb? Will you come to the meeting?

We often use 'will' with words like: probably, I think, I'm sure, tomorrow, next year.

examples.sentence: I think it will rain tomorrow.
I will help you with your homework.
She won't be at the meeting today.
Will you call me later?
I'm sure they will enjoy the trip.

questions.text: I think she ___ (will) like this gift.
He ___ (will, negative) be at school tomorrow.
___ you help me with this bag?
I'm sure they ___ (will) win the match.
Don't worry, I ___ (will) be there on time.
They ___ (will) probably move to a new city next year.

title: Modals: must / should
explanation:
'Must' expresses strong obligation or necessity - something is required: You must wear a seatbelt.

'Mustn't' (must not) expresses prohibition - something is not allowed: You mustn't smoke here.

'Should' expresses advice or recommendation - something is a good idea, but not required: You should drink more water.

'Shouldn't' (should not) expresses that something is not a good idea.

Both must and should are followed by the base form of the verb, without 'to'.

examples.sentence: You must wear a seatbelt in the car.
You mustn't smoke in the hospital.
You should study more for the exam.
She shouldn't eat so much sugar.
Students must arrive on time.

questions.text: You ___ wear a helmet when riding a motorcycle. (rule)
You ___ see a doctor if you feel sick. (advice)
Students ___ bring their ID card to the exam. (rule)
Choose the sentence with strong prohibition.
I think you ___ (should) apologize to her.

title: Object Pronouns (me, you, him, her, it, us, them)
explanation:
Object pronouns replace a noun that receives the action of the verb (the object of the sentence).

Subject -> Object: I -> me, you -> you, he -> him, she -> her, it -> it, we -> us, they -> them.

Object pronouns come after the verb, or after a preposition.

Compare: 'He loves her' (her = object, receives the action) with 'She loves him' (she = subject, him = object).

examples.sentence: She called me yesterday.
I saw him at the market.
Can you help us, please?
I gave the book to her.
We invited them to the party.

questions.text: Can you give this letter to ___? (he)
The teacher helped ___ with the homework. (they)
Look at that cat. Do you see ___?

title: Conjunctions: and, but, because, so
explanation:
Conjunctions connect words, phrases, or sentences.

'And' adds information: I like tea and coffee.

'But' shows contrast: I like tea, but I don't like coffee.

'Because' gives a reason: I stayed home because I was sick.

'So' shows a result: I was tired, so I went to bed early.

Note the difference between 'because' (gives the reason) and 'so' (gives the result): I was sick, so I stayed home = I stayed home because I was sick.

examples.sentence: She likes apples and oranges.
He is tired, but he wants to finish the work.
I didn't go out because it was raining.
It was raining, so I didn't go out.
She studied hard, so she passed the exam.

questions.text: She is rich, ___ she isn't happy.
I was late ___ the bus didn't come.
It was cold, ___ I wore a jacket. (result)
He failed the exam ___ he didn't study.
She was hungry, ___ she ate a sandwich. (result)
I want to go, ___ I don't have time.
We stayed inside ___ it was raining heavily.
I was tired, ___ I went to bed early.

title: Quantifiers: some, any, much, many
explanation:
We use 'some' in affirmative sentences and offers/requests: I have some money. Would you like some tea?

We use 'any' in negative sentences and questions: I don't have any money. Do you have any questions?

We use 'much' with uncountable nouns (singular): How much time do we have? There isn't much sugar left.

We use 'many' with countable nouns (plural): How many books do you have? There aren't many people here.

examples.sentence: I have some friends in London.
I don't have any brothers.
Do you have any free time today?
There isn't much water in the bottle.
How many languages do you speak?

questions.text: I have ___ apples in my bag.
I don't have ___ money right now.
How ___ sugar do you want in your tea?
How ___ books did you read this year?
There aren't ___ (many) chairs in the room.
There isn't ___ (much) milk in the fridge.
She bought ___ new clothes for the trip.
